observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. Municipal Accountant i. exert more time and effort to take up in the accounts of the municipality all valid book reconciling items, duly substantiated with appropriate documentation; ii. make the necessary adjustments in the books for long outstanding/stale checks to correct the Cash in Bank-LCCA and payable account balances; and iii. prepare JEVs to record the necessary adjusting entries to correct errors in recording collections; and b. Municipal Treasurer i. in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, properly identify the book reconciling items which concern the Treasury Office. Once these items are duly identified, forward to the Municipal Accountant the necessary reports and supporting documents for the preparation of adjusting journal entries; ii. locate the payees of the staled checks and inform them of the status of their checks so that appropriate action may be taken to adjust the accounts accordingly; iii. send written notice to the concerned payees at least one month before a check becomes stale and observe the proper cancellation and reporting of stale check in compliance with Section 59 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; iv. institute the preparation of Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD) on the day of actual collection, regardless of whether the collections are deposited within the day or not, and submit all RCDs and supporting documents to the Accountant for the preparation of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) pursuant to Section 29 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I, and Section 49 and Annex 41 of MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II; v. require all collectors and liquidating officer to remit religiously their collections on a daily basis, or in the case of the collectors assigned in the field, where travel time from their places of assignment to the Treasurer’s Office is more than one day, turnover of collections shall be made at least once a week or as soon as their collections reach ₱5,000; and vi. ensure that all collections are deposited intact in accordance with Section 28 of COA Circular No. 92-382, or whenever there is an opportunity to transact with the bank.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the
Municipal Treasurer undertake the following actions:
a. Coordinate with the bank to secure Credit (CM) and Debit (DM) Memo
advices, and prepare the necessary journal entries to record/ adjust the
valid reconciling items;
b. Promptly investigate and identify all unidentified reconciling items, secure
relevant supporting documents and prepare the appropriate adjusting
entries to ensure the accuracy of the CIB-LCCA balance; and
c. Prepare the necessary journal entry to reverse the cancelled check and
restore the CIB-LCCA balance, and initiate appropriate corrections in the
Bank Reconciliation Statement (BRS) to reflect the correct CIB-LCCA.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer (a) prepare and maintain SLs and Cashbooks, respectively, for the
accounts specified in Table 4; (b) reconcile the Cashbook balances with SL of
accounting on a regular basis in compliance with Section 143 of the LTOM, 2nd
Edition, so that discrepancies or errors could be immediately identified and
corrected; and (c) submit to the Audit Team the reconciled SLs and Cashbooks for
all Cash–Local Treasury and CIB-LCCA as of December 31, 2024, without further
delay.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
a. maintain RAAF for all types of accountable forms requisitioned by the
LGU, including commercial checks, to facilitate recording and monitoring of the
receipt, issue and transfer thereof; and
b. enhance his efforts to verify the Report of Collections and Deposits (RCD)
by checking the RAAF as to initial balances on hand, receipts, issues and the ending
balances on hand; making a physical count of the accountable forms remaining in
the custody of the collector and check the same against the new balances on hand
column; and reconciling the monthly RAAF with daily RCDs once a month, or as
often as needed, and immediately investigate and correct any discrepancy.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Local Chief Executive, through the
Municipal Accountant and the Inventory Committee Head, implement the One-Time
Cleansing of PPE account balances of the Municipality pursuant to the guidelines
and procedures prescribed under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31,
2020 in order to address the perennial issues of discrepancies in the PPE accounts
such as disparity in balances among PPE reports and records and unknown
whereabouts, conditions and other relevant information relative to PPE items, while
establishing accuracy of PPE balances and enforcing accountability to the concerned
officers and employees of the municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. transfer the completed projects to appropriate PPE accounts pursuant to
Section 50 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I;
b. provide depreciation for all completed projects in accordance with IPSAS
17; and
c. maintain CIPLC for each construction in progress to record the amount paid
per billing of the contractor in case of construction by straight contract, and the
amount paid for materials, labor and overhead in case of construction by
administration to facilitate regular monitoring and analysis of CIP accounts and
ensure that these are transferred to appropriate asset accounts once they are
completed and available for use.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entries to reclassify and close
the remaining tangible items with cost below ₱50,000.00 capitalization
threshold and the corresponding accumulated depreciation to Prior
Period Adjustment and Government Equity accounts, pursuant to COA
Circular Nos. 2022-004 and 2024-006 so as to effect the retrospective
application of the new accounting policy and correct the PPE and other
related balances; and
b. Supply and/or Property and Accounting Divisions/Units maintain the
appropriate forms, registry, and reports as prescribed under the
Circulars to strengthen internal controls over the semi-expendable
property.

We recommended that the:
a. Inventory Committee regularly conduct physical count of inventories and
prepare the corresponding RPCI in compliance with existing rules;
b. Municipal Accountant, in coordination with the concerned departments and
Inventory Committee, identify the whereabouts and condition of the
unsupported inventory items totaling ₱949,366.00. Once these items are duly
verified, prepare journal entries to record necessary adjustments and ensure that
the JEVs are supported with complete and valid documentation; and
c. Management (i) facilitate the reconciliation of the results of the physical
count with the accounting records to enable the recording of necessary
adjustments and ensure the accurate valuation of inventories in the books of
accounts, and (ii) institute measures to ensure the timely conduct of annual
physical inventory and submission of the corresponding report.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Accountant (i) regularly send demand letter to all AOs including
those who were separated from the service at least quarterly to demand the
settlement of their outstanding cash advances; (ii) ensure that no new cash
advance is granted unless previous ones have been fully liquidated; and (iii)
monitor LGU officials and employees who are due for
transfer/separation/retirement and ensure that clearance for money and
property accountability should not be issued unless they have first settled
their cash advances.
b. Management to consider the filing of appropriate legal or administrative
action against AOs who have deliberately failed to liquidate their advances
after proper demand, if warranted.

We recommended that the: a. Local Chief Executive enjoin the Local Finance Committee, Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC), Municipal Engineer and other implementing departments to: (i) consider in the planning and budgeting phases the prioritization of relevant, responsive and feasible PPAs to ensure that they are well-planned and procurement-and-implementation-ready; and (ii) strategically develop mechanisms to optimize and closely monitor the utilization and implementation of priority projects for the realization of socio-economic development and environmental outcomes; b. MPDC: (i) conduct a thorough planning for the implementation of development projects by considering the appropriate timing and phasing of their implementation; (ii) in coordination with the concerned offices, such as the Office of the Municipal Engineer, determine the specific works to be done thereon to avoid delays in the implementation; and (iii) henceforth identify and include specific dates under the schedule of implementation of each PPA in the AIP or individual project profile and fast track their implementation based on these scheduled time frames; c. Municipal Engineer, during the budget preparation for the ensuing year, prepare the Program of Work (PoW) and Detailed Estimates for infrastructure projects prior to inclusion of the PPAs in the AIP; and d. Municipal Accountant prepare necessary adjustments in the books for erroneous recording of fund transfers to barangays totaling ₱1,700,000.00 to correct the Due to LGUs and Water Supply Systems account balances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the: a. MDRRM Council and the MDRRM Officer i. refrain from charging office equipment, furniture and fixtures for Municipal DRRM Office’s use against the LDRRMF and that appropriations therefor should be sourced from administrative use from the General Fund; ii. adhere to the provisions of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1 in allocating LDRRMF solely for the implementation of the DRRM-related programs, projects, and PPAs; iii. prepare and align the programs, projects and activities that could address the four thematic areas of disaster risk reduction and management, namely Disaster Prevention and Mitigation, Disaster Response, and Disaster Preparedness, Disaster Rehabilitation and Recovery; and iv. observe proper planning in the conduct of DRRM-related trainings/seminars by assessing training needs beforehand, thereafter create a training plan which includes the Schedule of Training and Budget Estimates for training materials, meals and snacks, honoraria to lecturer/coordinator/facilitator and other related expenses, subject to the approval of the LCE. b. Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant i. charge the expenses against the LDRRMF in accordance with the duly approved LDRRM Plan; and ii. monitor the available funds for each program to avoid excess of expenditures over the legally authorized amount approved by the local appropriating authority for the programs in the same fiscal year. c. Municipal Accountant i. stop the practice of paying goods and services through reimbursement, except petty purchases to be paid out of the petty cash fund or through cash advance; and ii. thoroughly review the disbursement vouchers to determine completeness of supporting documents pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012. d. Local Chief Executive cause the submission of all transaction documents for the purchase of utility van with a cost of ₱1,319,000.00 and the lacking documents indicated in Table 7.6 of the AAR.
observations.managementAction:
Excluded the procurement of office equipment, furniture and fixtures, etc. for administrative use from the LDRRM Plan
Allocated the LDRRMF solely for the implementation of the DRRM-related activities
Aligned the PPAs with the Disaster Prevention and Mitigation, Disaster Preparedness, Disaster Response, and Disaster Rehabilitation and Recovery
Prepared Training Plan which contain, among others, the Activity Design, Schedule of Training, and Budgetary Requirements for the DRRM-related trainings/seminars conducted starting July 2024 onwards
Charged DRRM-related expenses against the LDRRMF in accordance with the plan
Monitored available funds for each PPA so as to avoid excess of expenditures over budget
Payment for goods and services is directed to the suppliers.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
Municipal Accountant (i) investigate and provide sufficient and appropriate details and supporting documents for all reconciling items in the BRS with emphasis on those inadequately labelled items or those plainly tagged as “unreconciled” in order to ensure their propriety and thereafter make the necessary adjustments in the books; (ii) be mindful of the monthly reconciliation of books and bank records and investigate the unusual accumulation of unrecorded deposits and disbursements; (iii) verify the validity of the long outstanding DIT and effect the necessary corrections if found to have been due to accounting errors; (iv) prepare BRS for each depository account of the Municipality;  and (v) prepare and maintain SLs for all bank accounts and immediately submit to the Audit Team the required subsidiary schedules of GL balances of cash accounts for all funds and SLs per depository bank;
Municipal Treasurer regularly (i) submit to the Accounting Office the financial transaction documents and related reports to facilitate its timely recording in the books of accounts; and (ii) regularly reconcile cashbook balance with accounting records to ensure the accuracy of the CIB-LCCA account balances; and
Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant coordinate with each other’s offices and identify the whereabouts of the transaction documents (e.g. duly approved DVs, RCDs, validated deposit slips) pertaining to the unrecorded deposits and disbursements. For documents still in the possession of the Treasury Office, immediately transmit the same to the Accounting Office for proper recording in the books and its subsequent submission to the Audit Team for audit purposes.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Treasurer (i) provide the Municipal Accountant with a certified list of all real property tax delinquencies, the format of which is provided in LTO Form 90, as the Municipal Accountant’s basis in taking up in the books the unrecorded tax delinquencies; and (ii) submit to the Municipal Accountant at the start of the year a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year as basis for setting-up RPT/SET Receivables; and
Municipal Accountant adopt the accrual basis of accounting in recognizing RPT and SET Receivables by ensuring the proper setting up of RPT/SET Receivables and their corresponding reciprocal accounts at the beginning of the year based on Treasury Office’s duly certified list of real property taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Accountant (i) through the Municipal Accountant, serve written notices to all concerned accountable officers requiring immediate settlement of their outstanding cash advances and institute legal action against them, if warranted; (ii) ensure that all concerned officers and employees strictly comply with the guidelines of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to forestall accumulation of unliquidated balances; and
Municipal Accountant (i) closely monitor the recording of financial transactions and strengthen review of JEVs, CDJ/GJ and GLs to ensure that all financial transactions are properly and accurately captured and taken up in the books to prevent the occurrence of accounting errors and negative balances in the accounts; and (ii) identify the cause/s of the negative balances and adjust the same in the books of accounts duly supported by valid and complete documentation.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds, determine the cause/s of disparity in their balances and record the necessary adjustments in the books to present the correct balances in the financial statements.  Henceforth, review the correctness of accounting entries in the JEVs to ensure the accuracy of account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor require the members of the Inventory Committee to conduct periodic physical inventory count of supplies and submit the Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semester, respectively;
Municipal Accountant diligently maintain Supplies Ledger Cards for each type of supplies, crops and other inventory items, to record its acquisition, issuance and disposal; and
Property Officer maintain updated stock cards of inventories pursuant to Section 119 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

We recommended the following corrective measures:
Municipal Accountant (i) maintain PPELC for each class of PPE, RPLC for each real property and LRLC for local road networks and ensure that all ledger cards contain complete details/description of the property to facilitate proper reconciliation with the RPCPPE; and (iii) provide depreciation for depreciable PPE with aggregate cost of ₱6,882,682.51 in accordance with Paragraphs 43 and 71 of IPSAS 17; and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the:
Municipal Accountant (i) investigate and provide sufficient and appropriate details and supporting documents for all reconciling items in the BRS with emphasis on those inadequately labelled items or those plainly tagged as “unreconciled” in order to ensure the propriety of such items and thereafter make the necessary adjustments in the books; (ii) verify the validity of the long outstanding deposits in transit. If found to have been due to accounting errors such as double recording, effect the necessary adjustments duly supported by adequate documentation. If valid, coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer for proper reconciliatin of cashbook and report of collections and deposits with Cash Receipts Journal; (iii) make the necessary adjustments in the books for the long outstanding/stale checks in accordance with specific procedures embodied in Section 59 of the MNGAS, Volume I; (iv) ensure the judicious and regular preparation of BRS; and (v) be mindful of the mandated duties by seeing that financial transactions are completely and accurately recorded in the books within the prescribed period;
Municipal Treasurer (i) send written notices to the concerned payees at least one month before a check becomes stale; and (ii) regularly submit to the Accounting Office the financial transaction documents and related reports to facilitate its timely recording in the books of accounts; and
Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant coordinate with each other’s offices and identify the whereabouts of the transaction documents (e.g. duly approved DVs, RCDs, validated deposit slips) pertaining to the unrecorded deposits and disbursements. For documents still in the possession of the Treasury Office, immediately transmit the same to the Accounting Office for its proper recording in the books and subsequent submission to the Audit Team for audit purposes. Evolve a smooth working pattern between the two offices and conduct regular reconciliation of cashbook with accounting records to ensure the accuracy of the CIB-LCCA and related account balances.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Treasurer update the Real Property Tax Account Registers or Taxpayer’s Index Cards, prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant the certified list of taxpayers showing the current/prior years amount due and collectible at the beginning of every year as basis in setting up the RPT/SET receivables and deferred income accounts; and
b. Municipal Accountant (i) reconcile the recorded RPT and SET  Receivable and the Deferred RPT Income and Deferred SET Income accounts with the Treasury report on the unpaid taxes for the current year as well the uncollected taxes in prior years and thereafter update the said accounts balances; ii) analyse the accounting entries made to determine the causes of the abnormal balances of the RPT Receivable and Deferred RPT Income accounts amounting to ₱1,439,355.06 and ₱1,496,544.13 and the discrepancies between the RPT and SET Receivables and their reciprocal account balances and effect the necessary adjustments, as appropriate; (iii) ensure the proper setting up of RPT and SET Receivables and their corresponding reciprocal accounts at the beginning of the year based on Treasury Office’s duly certified list of taxpayers and the amounts due and collectible; and (iv) exercise caution in analysing and recording financial transactions to forestall frequent erroneous accounting entries as they could materially affect the accuracy and fair presentation of account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the:
LCE (i) through the Municipal Accountant, serve written notices to all concerned accountable officers requiring immediate settlement of their outstanding cash advances and institute legal action against them, if warranted; and (ii) ensure that all concerned officers and employees strictly comply with the guidelines of COA Circular No. 97-002 on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to forestall accumulation of unliquidated balances; and
Municipal Accountant (i) closely monitor the recording of financial transactions and strengthen review of JEVs,  CDJ/GJ and GLs to ensure that all financial transactions are properly and accurately captured and taken up in the books to prevent the occurrence of accounting errors and negative balances in the accounts.

We recommended that the:
a. Inventory Committee (i) immediately conduct the physical inventory of all municipal property; (ii) prepare the Report on the Physical Count of PPE and the Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network to be reconciled with the Property Cards, PPE Ledger Cards, Road Network Ledger Cards and other accounting records; and (iii) submit a copy thereof to the Audit Team;
b. Municipal Accountant (i) maintain PPELC for each class of PPE, RPLC for each real property and LRNLC for local road networks and ensure that all ledger cards contain complete details/description of the property to facilitate proper reconciliation with the RPCPPE and RPCLRN; (ii) provide depreciation for all depreciable PPE with aggregate cost of ₱69,960,821.85  in accordance with Paragraphs 43 and 71 of IPSAS 17 and submit to the Audit Team the updated lapsing schedule for depreciation expenses of all depreciable PPE; (iii) transfer to the appropriate PPE accounts the costs of eight completed infrastructure projects, thereafter compute and recognize depreciation expense thereof and maintain subsidiary ledgers for the CIP accounts; and (iv) transfer to the GF books the cost of completed projects booked as PPE under the TF totaling ₱8,591,138.12; and
c.  Municipal Treasurer, as the acting GSO, (i) maintain complete property cards with complete information; and (ii) maintain a local road inventory and road map and Local Road Network Property Card pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 23, 2015.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the reciprocal accounts Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds, determine the cause/s of disparity in their balances and effect the necessary adjustments in the books to present their correct balances in the financial statements.  Henceforth, review the correctness of accounting entries in the JEVs to ensure the accuracy of account balances in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer ensure timely and full remittance of all taxes withheld by the Municipal Government to the BIR to avoid penalties and legal charges.

We recommended that the:
a. Local Chief Executive, as the agency head, require the officials who are responsible for the late remittances to the PhilHealth to be personally liable for the surcharges incurred;
b.  Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant dutifully observe and implement the provisions of Section 36 of COA Circular No. 92-382 and the existing laws and regulations on the prompt remittances of amounts due to the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG to prevent imposition of charges and penalties and to ensure continued availment of benefits of the employees; and
c.   Municipal Accountant:
1.  determine the specific transactions which caused the Due to Pag-IBIG account to perennially register abnormal month-end and year-end balances and immediately effect the necessary corrections supported by sufficient documentation; and
2.  make the necessary adjustments to correct the erroneuos recording of government’s share premiums to Due to GSIS and Due to Pag-IBIG accounts and henceforth thoroughly review and analyze financial transactions  to ensure the correctness of accounting entries.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) maintain separate SLs for each fund transfer and submit the same to the Audit Team for audit guidance and reference; (b) effect the necessary adjustment to correct the double recording of fund transfer from the DILG amounting to ₱9,735,000.00 and thereafter submit to the Audit Team the original copy of the JEV therefor together with JEV No. 300-19-12-gj05 as one of its supporting documents; (c) determine the cause of the variance between the ending balance of the Due to NGAs account and the subsidiary schedule as well as the negative balance of ₱31,808.00 in the GF and effect the necessary adjustments; and (d) submit to the Audit Team the transaction documents on the utilization of the fund transfer amounting to ₱9,735,000.00 to facilitate its liquidation with the DILG pursuant to COA Circular 94-013 dated December 13, 1994

We recommended that the:
a.  LCE, as the head of the agency ensure that the 20% DF is optimally utilized to help achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes of the Municipality in accordance with the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-1; and
b. Local Development Council, the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Budget Officer ensure that the projects to be funded under the 20% DF are aligned with those allowable development projects that partake the nature of investment and capital expenditures as numerated in Section 3.0 of the said JMC.

a.  LCE immediately coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan to secure the latter’s authority to open an STF bank account for the unexpended/unobligated balance of the DRRMF from the GF and the DRRM funds received from the National Government and other sources; and (b) thereafter, instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the LDRRMF unexpended balance to the STF pursuant to Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121 to ensure that funds are available for disaster risk reduction and management activities;
b.  LDRRM  Officer (i)  submit  the  Monthly  Report on  Sources  and Utilization of the DRRMF duly certified correct by the Municipal Accountant in accordance with the prescribed deadline stated in Item 5.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-002; and (ii) submit the LDRRMFIP for CY 2019 to the Audit Team; and
c.  Municipal Accountant  provide  adequate  disclosure  on the LDRRMF in the NTFS using the prescribed format in Annex D of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (i) transfer the remaining SET collections for CY 2019 to the SEF; (ii) prepare separate reports of collections for RPT and SET; and (iii) automatically deposit the proceeds of the additional 1% RPT accruing to the SEF depository account pursuant to Sections 82 and 86 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I; and (iv) submit to the Accounting Office the disbursement voucher and supporting documents pertaining to the transfer to the SEF amounting to ₱6,176,508.55 to facilitate its proper recording in the GF books, if the same is still in custody of the Treasury Office. If the same had already been forwarded to the Accounting Office, provide the Audit Team with a copy of the transmittal letter duly received by the Accounting Office.

We recommended that the:
Municipal Mayor, as the one who is primarily responsible for all government funds and property, ensure that required reports and documents are submitted by the concerned municipal government officials in such form and within the period prescribed by this Commission pursuant to the provisions of Sections 39 and 122 of P.D. No. 1445; and
Municipal Accountant prepare and submit the required financial statements, supporting schedules, and other reports in such form and within the period prescribed in the NGAS Manual for LGUs, COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010 and Paragraph 21 of the IPSAS 1.

We recommended that the:
a.  Municipal Treasurer instruct the Special Disbursing Officer to ensure that the liquidation of cash advances are covered by Report of Disbursements in compliance with Section 46 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Item 5.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002;
b.  Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant ensure that payments are made by check particularly those with material amounts; and
c. Municipal Accountant (a) thoroughly review the disbursements and liquidations and ensure that all disbursement vouchers and liquidation of cash advances are duly supported with valid proofs of receipts of payments; (b) ensure the correctness of accounting entries, the completeness of supporting documents and signify approval of the JEVs only after diligent review; and (c) ensure that journals are prepared in accordance with the prescribed format of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II and that each sheet is carefully reviewed and certified, footed and the totals are carried forward to the next sheet. Succeeding sheets shall start with the totals brought forward.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Mayor (a) demand payment from E.M. Abin Trading and Construction and Beta-J Construction for cost deficiencies totaling ₱401,150.50 and ₱32,615.09, respectively; and (b) be mindful of DPWH Department Order No. 102 dated November 8, 1988, re: Administrative Action on Contracts with Negative Slippages to ensure timely and effective remedial steps in response to delays in project implementation, otherwise, liquidated damages shall be imposed in accordance with Section 8, Annex “E” of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184. Be reminded also that concerned officials can be held personally liable for the amount for failing to take necessary measures to ensure that the project is implemented in accordance with the approved plans and schedules; and
b. Municipal Engineer submit a revised PERT/CPM and/or Bar Chart/S- curve showing the projected and actual physical accomplishments as of the contract expiry dates of November 18, 2017 for the “Construction of Drainage, Sidewalk, Curbs and Gutter and Construction/Improvement of Busuanga at Quezon and Salvacion Street” and January 5, 2018 for the “Construction/Improvement of Busuanga River Cruise Floating Restaurant and Boat Landing” project.

We recommended that the:

Local Chief Executive (LCE) require the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to (i) immediately reconcile their records as of December 31, 2018; and (ii) submit a reconciliation statement thereon and take-up the necessary adjusting entries in their respective records;

Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant strictly observe the provisions of Section 181 of the GAAM, Volume I and Section 63 of the LTOM on the required reconciliation of cashbook balances with subsidiary accounting records as these are important control tools for cash; and 

Municipal Accountant (i) prepare and update GL and maintain SL for all cash accounts; (ii) reconcile bank statements with accounting records and provide valid and complete details and supporting documents for all reconciling items before making necessary adjustments in the books; (iii) immediately submit to the Audit Team the required subsidiary schedule of GL balances of cash accounts for all funds, SLs per depository bank and Bank Reconciliation Statements supported with bank statements and valid bank debit/credit advices; and (iv) strictly comply with the provisions provided in the MNGAS Manual for LGUs and COA Circular No. 96-011.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following: 

Local Chief Executive (LCE) should create an Inventory Committee, consisting of a representative from the Office of the Municipal Mayor, Treasurer’s Office and Accounting Office and require each department head to fast track inventory of all properties under their office and submit the inventory report to the committee for validation and verification of the correctness and existence of the property.

The inventory committee should check the condition of the property, any damage, obsolete and unserviceable property shall be reported in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable and Waste Material Report and immediately request for disposal from the Commission.  

The inventory committee to fully accomplish the RPCPPE, indicating the actual count and complete details, and the results of the actual count shall be reconciled with the property and accounting records. All discrepancies between physical and book inventories must be investigated, adjusted and reconciled immediately.  From thereon, perform periodic inventory of PPE and prepare report on the results thereof, in accordance with 
existing regulations.  

The LCE should require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain ledger cards for each class and category of  property and equipment.  Work back on the previous years, based on the delivery receipt, acceptance and inspection report and other source documents and prepare property ledger card indicating acquisition date, cost and the brief descriptions which serves as the subsidiary ledgers of fixed assets per classs and category.  For the purpose of reconciling the accounting records  the reported balance of PPE per Balance Sheet is the based amount of property per category.  Based on ledger cards prepare lapsing schedule for the computation and monitoring of depreciation charges.

The Treasurer’s Office to maintain Property Cards per class and category of property, plant and equipment indicating acquisition date, the brief descriptions work back on the previous years and reconcile with the records of the accounting office to determine the correctness of the property records.  Update the property issue slip/PAR to check custodianship of the property.

a. prepare a schedule identifying the portion of tax remittances made during the year applicable to the GF, SEF and TF and effect the necessary adjustments, as appropriate;
b. maintain separate and updated subsidiary ledgers per fund for every kind of tax to facilitate prompt and correct remittance to the BIR; and
c. trace the accounting entries to the source documents in order to identify the cause of the abnormal account balances and effect the necessary adjustments, as appropriate.

During the audit exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer commented that the LGU is up to date in payment of taxes withheld. The Municipal Accountant committed to trace the erroneous accounting entries to its source documents and effect the necessary adjusting journal entries.

We recommended that the:
1. Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant:
a. observe and implement the provisions of Section 36 of COA Circular No. 92-382 and the existing laws and regulations on remittances of amounts due to the GSIS, PhilHealth and Pag-IBIG; and
b. remit promptly all the collections due to the concerned government agencies to prevent imposition of charges and penalties and to ensure continued availment of benefits of the employees.
2. Municipal Accountant:
a. maintain SLs for the Due to GSIS and Due to PhilHealth accounts; and
b. determine the causes of the huge negative or abnormal balances of the Due to GSIS and Due to Pag-IBIG accounts and effect the necessary corrections thereof.

During the audit exit conference, the Municipal Accountant commented that the withheld amounts for the mandatory deductions were not recorded unless liquidation of CAs for salaries and wages were submitted to the Accounting Office. Payment of remittances were debited to the corresponding liability accounts even though no amount of liability have yet been recognized due to the practice of the Accounting Office of recognizing the expenses for the mandatory deductions only upon submission of the liquidation reports. Nevertheless, the Municipal Accountant vowed to correct the practice and trace the accounting entries from its source documents in order to determine the cause of the negative balance.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant (a) maintain separate subsidiary ledgers for each fund transfer and submit the same to the Audit Team; and (b) determine the cause of the variance between the ending balance of the Due to NGAs account as well as the negative balance in the GF amounting to ₱31,808.00 and effect the necessary adjustments.

There was no written reply from the management. However, during the exit conference, the Municipal Accountant committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the required reports and documents in such form and within the period prescribed in the NGAS Manual for LGUs and COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.

Aside from the limited manpower in the Accounting Office, the Municipal Accountant stated that the delayed submission of DVs and supporting documents by the Municipal Treasurer contributed to the delayed submission of accounting reports and FSs. However, the Municipal Accountant remarked that she already discussed with the Municipal Treasurer how to expedite the submission of disbursement vouchers to facilitate the recording process in order to meet the timely submission of transaction documents to the Audit Team.

The management also commented that they will provide budget for additional accounting personnel in order to augment the existing manpower of the Accounting Office.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace, locate and recover the documents related to the above accounts and record the details obtained in the respective subsidiary ledgers.

Henceforth, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure that the Municipal Accountant maintains the required books of accounts including subsidiary ledgers pursuant to Section 114(2) of P.D. 1445 and submits the required subsidiary schedules of GL balances and subsidiary ledgers to substantiate the account balances reported in the financial statements pursuant to Section 347 of R.A. No. 7160 and Sections 10, 70 and 72 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I and Section 10 of the same Manual, Volume II.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to (a) compel the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to immediately submit the disbursement vouchers, obligation requests and supporting documents for the subject disbursements since the Municipal Accountant already recognized them in the books; and (b) henceforth, ensure strict adherence to the provisions of Section 347 of R.A. No. 7160 and Section 7.2.1(a) of COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the:
1. LCE, (a) thru the Municipal Accountant, submit immediately the duly authenticated copies of the required supporting documents enumerated above; and (b) expedite the transfer of ownership in favor of the Municipality of Busuanga; and
2. Municipal Accountant thoroughly review the disbursements and its attachments to determine completeness of supporting documents as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012 	6 001 dated June 14, 2012.

In the management reply dated August 30, 2019, the Municipal Mayor had immediately directed the Municipal Assessor to obtain the documents required to establish the validity of the transaction, ownership and valuation of the land purchased by LGU Busuanga. As of this date, only the tax clearance was submitted by the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer (a) transfer the remaining SET collections for CY 2018 to the SEF; (b) prepare separate reports of collections for RPT and SET; and (c) automatically deposit the proceeds of the additional 1% RPT accruing to the SEF depository account, in compliance with Sections 82 and 86 of the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume I.

The Municipal Treasurer committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Budget Officer (i) update and deduct the total declared savings from the appropriate registry of appropriation of specific funding source since these were already re-appropriated to new PPAs for year 2018; (ii) prepare accurate SAAOB, both current and continuing, and thoroughly check all the figures/amounts of appropriations, obligations and balances per PPA before submitting the report to the Municipal Accountant; and (iii) maintain complete registries in accordance with Section 18 of the MNGAS for LGUs, Volume II; and
b. Municipal Accountant and the Budget Officer (i) investigate the variances noted and effect adjustments in the accounting records and update the RAAO; and (ii) henceforth, reconcile their records on allotments and obligations monthly and any resulting discrepancies be immediately verified and corrected.

The management replied that, through the Municipal Budget Officer, they committed to submit an updated and corrected SAAOB to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the:
1. LCE, thru the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Engineer, the BAC and BAC Secretariat, (i) cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents which are necessary in the technical evaluation and validation of the projects; (ii) require the concerned officials to render satisfactory explanation for the deficiencies noted in audit; and (iii) henceforth, ensure strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012 on the required supporting documents;
2. Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer compute and impose liquidated damages against the two contractors of the 3 infrastructure projects which were completed beyond contract expiry date pursuant to Section 8 of the Contract Implementation Guidelines for the Procurement of Infrastructure Projects.
3. BAC strictly abide by the standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its Revised I.R.R. and other relevant laws on government procurement; and
4. Municipal Accountant ensure completeness of supporting documents before processing and certifying the DVs.

During the audit exit conference, the management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the LCE, as the head of the agency (a) direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to observe strict compliance with the provisions of Section 344 of R.A. No. 7160 regarding their mandated functions in the disbursement of government funds; and (b) stop the practice of allowing personnel holding first level positions to perform the tasks of those holding second-level positions pursuant to Sections 13(c)(2) and 13(c)(6), Rule IV of the ORAOHRA.

We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Engineer (ME) and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) (i) reevaluate and complete the list of infrastructure projects in the monitoring report and provide all the minimum required information on the implementation of each project; and (ii) immediately submit to the Audit Team the consolidated status report/information of all the infrastructure projects started, completed and still on-going as of December 31, 2018;
b. Municipal Engineer hasten the implementation of all on-going and unimplemented projects and make sure that all infrastructure projects are strictly monitored and implemented in accordance with plans and schedules so that the intended beneficiaries could enjoy the benefits of these programs; and
c. LCE, ME and MPDC strictly comply with the guidelines of COA Circular No. 2013-004 regarding information and publicity of PPAs as well as the general provisions of succeeding GAAs on transparency in the status of implementation of PPAs.

During the audit exit conference, the Municipal Engineer committed to update and regularly submit the required reports.

We recommended that the:
1. LDRRM Officer (a) submit the Monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of the DRRMF duly certified correct by the Municipal Accountant in accordance with the prescribed deadline stated in Item 5.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-002; and (b) submit the LDRRMFIP for CY 2018 to the Audit Team;
2. Municipal Accountant submit the required disclosures of the LDRRMF in the NTFS; and
3. LCE immediately coordinate with the Sangguniang Bayan to secure the latter’s authority to open an STF bank account for the unexpended/unobligated balance of the DRRMF from the GF and the DRRM funds received from the National Government and other sources; and (b) instruct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to transfer the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF to a special trust fund pursuant to Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121 to ensure that funds are available for disaster risk reduction and management activities.

During the audit exit conference, the Municipal Treasurer remarked that an authority shall be secured from the Sangguniang Bayan for the opening of a separate depository account for the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF. The LDRRM Officer commented that the report on LDRRMF utilization is regularly submitted to the DILG but he was unaware that it has to be submitted to COA. Nevertheless, he committed to regularly submit to the Audit Team the Report on Sources and Utilization of the LDRRMF as well as the LDRRMFIP.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as head of the GFPS (a) ensure that the total appropriation provided for GAD PPAs in the Annual and Supplemental Budget of any given year is in line and supported with DILG-approved GPB; and (b) cause the preparation of GAD AR strictly in accordance with the guidelines set forth in PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1.

During the audit exit conference, the management stated that they will submit the Supplemental GPB together with the AR with itemized accomplishment per activity and they also committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor enjoin the BAC Secretariat for the timely submission of copies of government contracts and each of all the documents forming part thereof within five (5) working days from execution, and the designated Supply Officer should furnish the COA of the POs within five (5) working days from issuance date pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 to ensure timely review and evaluation of the same so that management could be immediately informed of any defects/deficiencies noted thereon.
observations.managementAction: Although incomplete, there were several contracts and supporting documents, all photocopies, submitted in the 3rd quarter of CY 2018.

Hence, this observation was reiterated in Finding No. 16 of this AAR.

observations.recommendation:

Present to the Local Finance Committee the unimplemented PPAs and the balances of continuing appropriations every end of the year for further review and for possible re-programming and inclusion in the annual budget preparation. We recommended the following:
We reiterate that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) should create an Inventory Committee, consisting of a representative from the Office of the Municipal Mayor, Treasurer’s Office and Accounting Office.
Require each department head to fast track inventory of all properties under their office and submit the inventory report to the committee for validation and verification of the correctness and existence of the property.
The inventory committee should check the condition of the property, any damage, obsolete and unserviceable property shall be reported in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable and Waste Material Report and immediately request for disposal from the Commission.
The inventory committee to fully accomplish the RPCPPE, indicating the actual count and complete details, and the results of the actual count shall be reconciled with the property and accounting records. All discrepancies between physical and book inventories must be investigated, adjusted and reconciled immediately.  From thereon, perform periodic inventory of PPE and prepare report on the results thereof, in accordance with existing regulations.
The LCE should require the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain ledger cards for each class and category of  property and equipment.  Work back on the previous years, based on the delivery receipt, acceptance and inspection report and other source documents and prepare property ledger card indicating acquisition date, cost and the brief descriptions which serves as the subsidiary ledgers of fixed assets per classs and category.  For the purpose of reconciling the accounting records  the reported balance of PPE per Balance Sheet is the based amount of property per category.  Based on ledger cards prepare lapsing schedule for the computation and monitoring of depreciation charges.
The Treasurer’s Office to maintain Property Cards per class and category of property, plant and equipment indicating acquisition date, the brief descriptions work back on the previous years and reconcile with the records of the accounting office to determine the correctness of the property records.  Update the property issue slip/PAR to check custodianship of the property.

We recommended that the management should impose salary deductions from those officials and employees who have not settled their cash advances for travel or for other purposes, as well as the institution of other sanctions, as provided for under Sections 5.1.3 and 5.9 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 16 of Executive Order 248.
The Local Chief Executive (LCE), the Municipal Accountant should strictly implement the Section 4.1.2 of the same COA Circular that no additional cash advance should be allowed unless the previous cash advance has been liquidated.
The Management should send Demand Letters to those separated from the service to immediately liquidate/settle their cash advances.  Likewise, initiate the filing of the request for write-off from the Commission on Audit pertaining to outstanding balances of deceased employees.

We recommended that the management to minimize the incurrence of meals and snacks to the extent allowed  by law and observe  economy and austerity measures in the use of government funds. Further, meals and snacks for entertaining visitors/guests of their corresponding offices were deemed proper to be charged to the monthly reperesentation allowance of the department heads. Lastly, the Accounting Office should record financial transactions in the proper accounts prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015.

We recommended that the Municipality with the concurrence of Sangguniang Bayan should formulate, adopt written guidelines on the grant of communication allowance.  Set a criterion for officials or employees who are entitled to the allowance based on their position/designation and the functions of their office and set a limit of the maximum amount each official can claim for his official mobile phone taking into consideration the provisions of COA Circular No. 2012-003.  Lastly, ensure that claims for communication expenses are duly supported with the required supporting documents or for practical reason the Municipality may fix the monthly cell phone allowance.

Lastly, the Municipal Planning and Development  Coordinator together with the Local Development Council should coordinate, monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development programs, projects and activities of the Municipality in accordance with the approved development plan and properly render a report to the Mayor the status of every project, problems encountered and of any recommendeded measures to address such problem.

The Local Chief Executive (LCE) should create an Inventory Committee, consisting of a representative from the Office of the Municipal Mayor, General Services Office (GSO) and Accounting Office.

Update the property issue slip/PAR to check custodianship of the property.

Prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and furnish the accounting office for any adjustment

The Municipality should drop the unserviceable and obsolete properties items from the books following the process prescribed by the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in Local Government under COA Circular No. 92-386.

observations.recommendation:
The audit team recommended that the Municipal Mayor should instruct all Department Heads to religiously perform their respective responsibilities in the pursuance of the accounting procedures for budgetary accounts as being enumerated in the foregoing paragraph. The Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant should reconcile monthly their records on allotments available pursuant to Section 10, Volume 1 of GAAM to ensure accurate reporting and reconciliation of Trial Balance and the SAAO.  Most importantly, the Municipal Accountant should ensure that all expenditures are charged appropriately to cost centers, programs/activities and projects in accordance with the budget.
We also recommended strict compliance with the provisions prescribed by Sec. 325 (a) of R.A. No. 7160 and LBC No. 98 dated October 14, 2011. The Municipal Budget Officer and the Local Finance Committee should perform a thorough review and evaluation of the budget, whether annual or supplemental, prior to its submission to the Sangguniang Bayan to ensure that all PS items are properly identified and classified as PS and that appropriations for PS items shall not exceed the PS limitation at any point in time during the budget year.  
To be able to implement and provide additional PS costs, the municipal government should make an effort in raising revenues/receipts or reducing its PS expenses. If it is inevitable to hire additional manpower, provide appropriations for salaries and wages of casual and job order employees under Personal Services. Otherwise, maximize the service of existing employees and minimize the hiring of casual and job order employees.
Moreover, stop the practice of charging and recording other personal services (PS) to MOOE.

 Demand letters to liquidate the cash advances be sent to the officers and employees with unliquidated cash advances

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the ICO-Municipal Treasurer should (a) deposit all collections promptly and intact with the authorized government depository bank, in compliance with Section 69 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 32 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I to avoid possible loss and misuse of funds; (b) properly maintain his cashbook by fund and regularly update, balance and reconcile his record with the cash on hand daily or as necessary; and (c) reconcile his cashbook with the records of the Municipal Accountant at least quarterly to ensure that the recorded transactions are complete, reliable, accurate and timely.

Lastly, the Municipal Accountant should segregate the balance of withdrawals from actual cash collections and reclassify the unsettled accountabilities to the appropriate account. Cash advances made by disbursing officers should be accurately recorded to the “Advances to Special Disbursing Officer” account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Accounting Department should reconcile records with the Treasury and the Assessor’s Office. The whole agency should join hand in order to determine and collect the right amount of RPT and SEF taxes from every real property owners within the municipality. They should send confirmation letters to the corresponding taxpayers in order to confirm actual balances due and receivable. This will also uncover possible unremitted collections which are common problems among other LGUs. Accounting should be required to provide and maintain subsidiary records for real property and special education tax receivables to account for the breakdown of the balances appearing in the general ledger. A schedule of these receivables indicating the taxpayers’ name and the amount due per year should be attached to the agency’s interim and yearend financial reports being submitted to the COA Auditor concerned. Above all, the Municipal Treasurer should exercise the remedies for the collection of delinquent taxes as provided for in Section 256, RA 7160.
observations.managementAction: No management reply

observations.recommendation:
In order to address the noted observations and increase collections, the Team recommended that management:

Formulate a clear cut policy (SB Ordinance) that will improve the assessment of business taxes such as: 

Require all business establishments with at least gross receipts of over P500, 000.00 per annum that the sworn statement of gross sales or receipts for the preceding calendar year should be supported with documents such as audited financial statements and BIR Business Tax Returns (VAT & Percentage Tax Remittance). 

For smaller firms and market, make use of presumptive minimum gross sales/receipts levels. 
 For lessors of Real Estate, sworn statements should at least be supported with notarized contracts of lease.
 Require that all real estate transfer tax payments must be accompanied by confirmation of payment of other taxes, including the business tax and mayors permit fees.

Require the ICO-Municipal Treasurer to inspect business establishments within the jurisdiction of the Municipality to ensure effective and efficient collection of business taxes as mandated under [Section 470 (d) (4)] of LGC.

observations.recommendation:
For all the observations noted we recommended the following:
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) should create an Inventory Committee, consisting of a representative from the Office of the Municipal Mayor, General Services Office (GSO) and Accounting Office.
The GSO should maintain Property Cards per class and category of property, plant and equipment indicating acquisition date, the brief descriptions and work back on the previous years and reconcile with the records of the accounting office to determine the correctness of the property records.  Update the property issue slip/PAR to check custodianship of the property.
The LCE should require each department head to fast track the inventory taking of all properties under their office and submit the inventory report to the committee for validation and verification of the correctness and existence of the property.
In the event that an inventory committee is created it should check the condition of the property, any damage, obsolete and unserviceable property shall be reported in the Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable and Waste Material Report and immediately request for disposal.  Prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and furnish the accounting office for any adjustment
The LCE should compel the inventory committee to fully accomplish the RPCPPE, indicating the actual count and complete details, and the results of the actual count shall be reconciled with the property and accounting records. All discrepancies between physical count and property records must be investigated, adjusted and reconciled immediately.  From thereon, perform periodic inventory of PPE and prepare report on the results thereof, in accordance with existing regulations.
The Municipality should drop the unserviceable and obsolete properties items from the books following the process prescribed by the Rules and Regulations on Supply and Property Management in Local Government under COA Circular No. 92-386.

We recommended that the management should impose salary deductions from those officials and employees who have not settled their cash advances for travel or for other purposes, as well as the institution of other sanctions, as provided for under Sections 5.1.3 and 5.9 of COA Circular No. 97-002 and Section 16 of Executive Order 248.
The Local Chief Executive (LCE), the Municipal Accountant should strictly implement Section 4.1.2 of the same COA Circular that no additional cash advance should be allowed unless the previous cash advance has been liquidated.
The Management should send Demand Letters to those separated from the service to immediately liquidate/settle their cash advances.  Likewise, initiate the filing of the request for write-off from the Commission on Audit pertaining to outstanding balances of deceased employees.
We recommended that the Municipality should be more prudent in the utilization of its LDRRMF, taking into consideration the existing rules and regulations governing the utilization of the said fund.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the reconciliation of actual amounts on a comparable basis and actual amounts in the financial statements and present it in the notes to financial statements or in face of the statement of comparison of budget and actual amounts as part of the financial statements.

We recommended that the local government to organize the Solid Waste Management Board (SWMB) and prioritized the formulation of the 10-year solid waste management plan that could guide the Municipality in performing a systematic approach in identifying feasible means towards the reduction of waste.  The management to cause the immediate establishment of “Sanitary Landfill” of the Municipality.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive (LCE) to instruct the Municipal Engineer to submit the list of on-going government PPAs and those to be implemented during the year.  The LCE should regularly submit the monitoring report and to submit at the beginning of the year a list of on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year. Strict compliance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 dated January 30, 2013.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should require the Local Development Council during the preparation of the annual budget and the 20% DF to include only PPAs which are feasible, specific and properly identified as to target date of execution and the personnel in-charge responsible for the implementation. Likewise, coordinate with the Municipal Engineering Office so that the same shall be supported with POWs to ensure optimum utilization of the funds and timely and efficient implementation  of the projects.
Present to the Local Finance Committee the unimplemented PPAs and the balances of continuing appropriations every end of the year for further review and for possible re-programming and inclusion in the annual budget preparation. Unexpended balances of appropriations of projects/programs/activities which were already completed/accomplished should be re-appropriated for other programs/projects/activities for the ensuing year.
Lastly, the Municipal Planning and Development  Coordinator together with the Local Development Council should coordinate, monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different development programs, projects and activities of the Municipality in accordance with the approved development plan and properly render a report to the Mayor the status of every project, problems encountered and of any recommended measures to address such problem.

We recommended for the Municipal Mayor to ensure that the budget for the ensuing years are prepared in accordance with the procedures, schedules and local budget preparation forms prescribed under the Budget Operations Manual for LGUs and for the Sangguniang Bayan to require, prior to the approval of the Annual and Supplemental Budgets, that special purpose appropriations are categorized by object of expenditure and presented in a similar sequence as a department or office expenditure program except the staffing and compensation profile where there are no personnel and office to operate.

We recommended that the Honorable Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and submit Subsidiary Schedules of GL accounts to substantiate the validity and correctness of the accounts, pursuant to Sections 111.1 and 114.2 of P.D. No. 1445 and Sections 2 and 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.

We recommended also that the Municipal Accountant strictly adhere to laws, rules and regulations pertaining to the submission of financial reports and schedules to the Commission on Audit (COA).

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor cause the strict adherence to laws, rules and regulations pertaining to the submission of financial statements, records and reports to the Commission on Audit (COA) from the ICO-Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant in order that all financial reports and transaction documents are submitted on time.

The Municipal Accountant should ensure the completeness of all the documents supporting the disbursements, as well as the signatures therein, before it is processed for payment. The ICO-Municipal Treasurer should secure additional copies of DVs for his use in updating records so as not to delay the preparation and submission of financial reports.



We also recommended immediate submission of all transaction documents for the period January to December 2015 to the Audit Team.
